title: What is the difference between switchMap, mergeMap, concatMap, and exhaustMap?
answer: All flatten inner observables but handle concurrency differently: **switchMap** cancels the previous inner (great for typeahead), **mergeMap** runs all concurrently, **concatMap** queues them in order, and **exhaustMap** ignores new emissions while one is active (great for preventing double-submits).

title: What is the difference between Subject, BehaviorSubject, and ReplaySubject?
answer: A **Subject** multicasts to subscribers but emits nothing on subscribe. A **BehaviorSubject** requires an initial value and gives new subscribers the latest value. A **ReplaySubject** replays a buffered number of past emissions to new subscribers.

title: What is content projection and ng-content?
answer: **Content projection** lets a component render markup passed between its tags via `<ng-content>`. Multi-slot projection uses `<ng-content select="...">` to place different projected pieces — enabling flexible, reusable components like cards and dialogs.

title: What do @HostListener and @HostBinding do?
answer: **@HostListener** subscribes to an event on the host element (e.g. `@HostListener('click')`); **@HostBinding** binds a host element's property/attribute/class/style to a component field. They're common in attribute directives to react to and style the host.

title: What is trackBy in ngFor and why use it?
answer: `trackBy` gives Angular a stable identity function for list items so it reuses DOM nodes instead of recreating them when the array changes — preventing flicker, preserving element state, and improving performance on large or frequently updated lists.

title: What is ViewEncapsulation in Angular?
answer: **ViewEncapsulation** controls how component styles are scoped: `Emulated` (default — Angular adds attributes so styles don't leak), `None` (global styles), and `ShadowDom` (native Shadow DOM isolation).

title: Why use Renderer2 instead of direct DOM access?
answer: **Renderer2** abstracts DOM manipulation so your code works across platforms (server-side rendering, web workers) and stays safe from XSS. Directly touching `nativeElement` couples you to the browser and is discouraged.

title: How do you dynamically create components?
answer: Use a **ViewContainerRef** and call `createComponent(MyComponent)` (modern API) to instantiate a component at runtime and insert it into the view. Useful for modals, dynamic dashboards, and rendering components chosen at runtime.

title: What is NgZone and when do you run outside Angular?
answer: **NgZone** integrates Zone.js so Angular knows when to run change detection. For high-frequency work (animations, scroll, third-party libs) you call `ngZone.runOutsideAngular(...)` to skip change detection, then `run(...)` to re-enter when you need the UI updated.

title: How do you create a custom validator in reactive forms?
answer: A **validator** is a function taking a control and returning `null` (valid) or an error object: `(c) => c.value === 'x' ? null : { invalid: true }`. Attach it in the `FormControl` config. Async validators return an Observable/Promise of the error object.

title: What is a FormArray?
answer: A **FormArray** manages a dynamic, indexed list of controls (or groups) — ideal for variable-length inputs like adding/removing multiple emails or rows. You `push`/`removeAt` controls and iterate them in the template.

title: How do you read route parameters?
answer: Inject **ActivatedRoute** and read `paramMap`/`queryParamMap` as observables (or snapshots). Observables are preferred when the same component is reused across param changes, so you react to navigation without re-instantiating.

title: What is Angular Universal / SSR?
answer: **SSR** (Angular Universal) renders the app's HTML on the server for the initial request, improving first paint, SEO, and perceived performance. The client then **hydrates** the markup to become interactive. Modern Angular has built-in hydration support.

title: What are preloading strategies?
answer: After the initial load, Angular can **preload** lazy modules in the background: `PreloadAllModules` loads everything, `NoPreloading` (default) loads on demand, and custom strategies preload selectively (e.g. only routes flagged for it) to balance speed and bandwidth.

title: What is an InjectionToken?
answer: An **InjectionToken** is a unique key for injecting values that aren't classes — config objects, primitives, or interfaces (which don't exist at runtime). You provide it with `{ provide: TOKEN, useValue: ... }` and inject it via `@Inject(TOKEN)` or `inject(TOKEN)`.

title: What is the difference between useClass, useValue, useFactory, and useExisting?
answer: Provider recipes tell DI how to create a dependency: **useClass** instantiates a class, **useValue** supplies a ready value, **useFactory** runs a function (with optional `deps`), and **useExisting** aliases another token. They enable mocking, configuration, and abstraction.

title: What does ChangeDetectorRef do?
answer: **ChangeDetectorRef** lets you control detection manually: `markForCheck()` schedules a check up the tree (used with OnPush), `detectChanges()` runs it now, and `detach()`/`reattach()` exclude/include a component — useful for performance-critical or detached views.

title: How do child components emit events to parents?
answer: A child declares `@Output() change = new EventEmitter<T>()` and calls `this.change.emit(value)`. The parent binds `(change)="handler($event)"`. Modern Angular also offers the `output()` function as a signals-based alternative.

title: What are template reference variables?
answer: A **template reference variable** (`#myInput`) names an element, component, or directive in the template so you can reference it elsewhere in that template (e.g. `myInput.value`) or query it with `@ViewChild('myInput')`.

title: What are ng-container and ng-template?
answer: `<ng-container>` is a grouping element that renders no DOM — useful for applying structural directives without adding a wrapper. `<ng-template>` defines a template that isn't rendered until instantiated (by `*ngIf`, `ngTemplateOutlet`, etc.).

title: What is ngTemplateOutlet?
answer: `*ngTemplateOutlet` renders a referenced `<ng-template>`, optionally passing a context object for its template variables. It enables reusable, customizable template snippets — e.g. letting a list component accept a custom item template from its parent.

title: What is NgRx?
answer: **NgRx** is a Redux-style reactive state library for Angular: a single immutable **store**, **actions** describing events, **reducers** producing new state, **selectors** for derived reads, and **effects** for side effects. It suits large apps with complex shared state.

title: How do you handle errors and retries globally with interceptors?
answer: In an interceptor, pipe the request with `catchError` to transform/log errors and `retry`/`retryWhen` to re-attempt transient failures (e.g. exponential backoff on 503), centralizing resilience and error handling across all HTTP calls.

title: How does two-way binding work under the hood?
answer: `[(x)]` ('banana in a box') is sugar for a property binding plus an event binding: `[x]="value"` and `(xChange)="value=$event"`. A component supports it by having an `@Input() x` and an `@Output() xChange` (the convention `ngModel` follows).

title: What are signal inputs and model() in modern Angular?
answer: `input()` declares a signal-based component input (read reactively, with optional required/transform), and `model()` creates a two-way bindable signal input that emits changes automatically — replacing `@Input`/`@Output` boilerplate in the signals era.

title: How do you avoid memory leaks from subscriptions?
answer: Prefer the `async` pipe (auto-unsubscribes). For manual subscriptions, unsubscribe in `ngOnDestroy`, use `takeUntil(destroy$)`, or the modern `takeUntilDestroyed()` operator. Leaked subscriptions keep components alive and cause memory and duplicate-handler bugs.

title: What does the Angular CLI provide?
answer: The **Angular CLI** scaffolds and manages projects: `ng new`, `ng generate` (components/services/etc.), `ng serve` (dev server), `ng build` (optimized prod build), `ng test`, and schematics for consistent code generation and updates (`ng update`).

title: How do you bootstrap a standalone Angular application?
answer: Standalone apps start with `bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, { providers: [...] })` instead of an `AppModule`. App-wide providers (router, HttpClient, etc.) are passed via the providers array using functions like `provideRouter` and `provideHttpClient`.

title: What is a route resolver and when is it useful?
answer: A **resolver** fetches data before a route activates so the component renders with everything ready (no loading flash for critical data). Modern Angular uses functional resolvers; the resolved data is read from `ActivatedRoute.data`.

title: What is the difference between providing a service in root vs in a component?
answer: `providedIn: 'root'` (or providing at the app level) creates one **singleton** shared everywhere. Providing a service in a component's `providers` array creates a **new instance per component instance** (and its children) — useful for component-scoped state that shouldn't be shared.
